title: Boundary Geometry Turns Entanglement into Steering
abstract: Entanglement does not in general imply Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen steering. We identify a boundary-geometric mechanism that closes this gap on product-null boundary strata of two-qubit state space, where Bob's conditional states touch the boundary of the Bloch ball. The key obstruction is local: if a projective assemblage approaches a Bloch-sphere boundary contact with a first-order tangential displacement but only a second-order inward defect, then no finite-measure local-hidden-state model can reproduce it. For two-qubit states with a product vector in the kernel, this boundary contact is exactly the tangency of Bob's steering ellipsoid to the Bloch sphere. At such a product-null tangency, a single tangential coherence controls both partial-transpose negativity and the boundary-contact scaling obstruction. The same boundary minor gives a compact experimental witness: once the product-null contact is verified or guaranteed, the tangential coherence supplies the steering signal. Consequently, every entangled two-qubit rank-two state, and every entangled rank-three state whose null space is spanned by a product vector, is two-way projectively steerable. The same boundary idea extends to arbitrary steering cuts: the Bloch-sphere contact is replaced by a rank-deficient trusted conditional state, and support-kernel first-order coherence implies both NPT entanglement and projective steering.
summary: The paper demonstrates that for certain classes of quantum states, entanglement and EPR steering are equivalent due to a geometric scaling obstruction at the state space boundary. It provides a practical experimental witness for steering by measuring tangential coherence in states where the conditional state touches the Bloch sphere boundary.
main_problem: Investigating the conditions under which the gap between entanglement and Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen (EPR) steering closes, specifically looking for a mechanism where entanglement implies steering.
main_result: The authors identify a boundary-geometric mechanism where, on the product-null boundary of the state space, entanglement is equivalent to two-way projective steering. This collapse of the hierarchy applies to all entangled two-qubit rank-two states and certain rank-three states.
method: The study uses boundary-scaling analysis of conditional states near the Bloch-sphere boundary and a mathematical proof involving the Radon-Nikodym theorem to demonstrate a scaling obstruction to Local Hidden State (LHS) models.
model_or_system: The study focuses on bipartite quantum systems (specifically two-qubit states and general cuts X|Y) involving an untrusted party (Alice) and a trusted party (Bob), modeled using projective assemblages and Local Hidden State (LHS) models.
key_observables: Tangential coherence (h02 or b_t), inward defect (delta), and the boundary witness (W_bd).
important_parameters: The product-null boundary regime, where the kernel of the density matrix contains a product vector, and the scaling regime defined by first-order tangential displacement and second-order inward defect.
main_assumptions: The primary results rely on the specific geometric condition of conditional states touching the boundary of the Bloch ball (product-null contact) and assume Alice's side is restricted to local unitaries.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates boundary contact and the quadratic inward defect of conditional states; Figure 2 shows the boundary data (populations and coherence) and the boundary witness used to detect the NPT minor.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the gap between entanglement and steering, introduces the product-null boundary geometry, provides a mathematical proof of the scaling obstruction to LHS models, extends the results to higher-dimensional/multipartite cuts, and concludes with an experimental witness.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for quantum information and quantum optics as it provides a new geometric way to certify steering and provides a method to upgrade entanglement witnesses into steering certificates using only local boundary data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Boundary contact and quadratic inward defect. (a) Product-null boundary contact at N, with shrinking caps Γt. (b) The normalized inward defect δ(u) is bounded by a quadratic curve Cu2 for a representative product-null fam- ily.

FIG. 2. Minimal boundary witness. (a) The boundary data consist of the product-null population p0, the auxiliary popu- lation p1, and the tangential coherence Ctan. (b) The witness Wbd = |Ctan|2 −p0p1 detects the negative partial-transpose minor. At the product-null boundary, the theorem upgrades this NPT witness to two-way projective steering.
